title: Parkinson disease after TBI: degeneration of neurons
vignette: A 40-year-old man comes to the office due to a 6-month history of sleep abnormalities, constipation, and generalized slowing of activity. In addition, he has difficulty rising from a seated position. Medical history is significant for a traumatic head injury 3 years ago, which required a prolonged hospital stay. The patient has no other medical conditions and takes no medication. He does not use tobacco, alcohol, or recreational drugs. Vital signs are normal. Examination shows resistance to passive movement in the upper extremities, which is slightly greater on the left than on the right. Left hand tremor and decreased arm swing occur during walking. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's current condition?

clues.note: Bradykinesia (slowness of movement) is the cardinal, required feature of parkinsonism. Together with rest tremor and rigidity it defines the syndrome.

clues.text: difficulty rising from a seated position
clues.note: Trouble standing up reflects bradykinesia and rigidity of axial/proximal muscles - a typical early Parkinson complaint.

clues.text: traumatic head injury 3 years ago, which required a prolonged hospital stay
clues.note: Significant TBI (especially with prolonged loss of consciousness) increases the incidence and hastens the onset of Parkinson disease - which is why this man is parkinsonian at only 40. It accelerates dopaminergic neurodegeneration.

clues.text: resistance to passive movement in the upper extremities, which is slightly greater on the left
clues.note: ASYMMETRIC rigidity (cogwheel) is characteristic of idiopathic Parkinson disease; symmetric findings would suggest drug-induced or atypical parkinsonism.

clues.text: Left hand tremor and decreased arm swing
clues.note: A resting tremor and reduced arm swing on one side complete the triad (tremor + rigidity + bradykinesia) and confirm asymmetric Parkinson disease.

whatMatters: Bradykinesia + asymmetric resting tremor + rigidity (plus prodromal REM sleep changes and constipation) = Parkinson disease, caused by DEGENERATION of dopaminergic NEURONS in the substantia nigra (with alpha-synuclein/Lewy bodies). Prior significant TBI accelerates this neurodegeneration, explaining onset at 40. Answer B.
strategy: Recognize the parkinsonian triad, then pick the cell that dies. Parkinson = loss of dopaminergic NEURONS (substantia nigra). Match the other options to their diseases: astrocyte swelling (acute TBI edema/hepatic), oligodendrocyte loss (demyelination/MS), endothelial disruption (vascular/diffuse axonal injury), vascular amyloid (cerebral amyloid angiopathy/Alzheimer). TBI here is a RISK accelerant, not a competing mechanism.

eliminations.label: Astrocyte swelling - acute injury/edema, not PD
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: astrocyte (cytotoxic) swelling occurs in acute TBI edema and in hyperammonemia (Alzheimer type II astrocytes). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it is an acute, early phenomenon and does not produce a chronic asymmetric parkinsonian syndrome years later. ✅ Correct if: acute cerebral edema/raised ICP or hepatic encephalopathy.

eliminations.label: Degeneration of oligodendrocytes - demyelination
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: oligodendrocytes make CNS myelin; their loss causes demyelination (e.g., multiple sclerosis). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: demyelination gives UMN/sensory/optic deficits, not a hypokinetic rest-tremor parkinsonism from nigral dopamine loss. ✅ Correct if: relapsing neurologic deficits with white-matter lesions.

eliminations.label: Disruption of vascular endothelial cells - DAI/vascular injury
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: endothelial/axonal shearing and blood-brain-barrier disruption occur in diffuse axonal injury and vascular disease. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: that explains acute post-traumatic deficits, not the delayed dopaminergic neurodegeneration causing this triad. ✅ Correct if: acute coma after rotational head trauma (DAI) or stroke.

eliminations.label: Vascular amyloid deposition - cerebral amyloid angiopathy
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: beta-amyloid in vessel walls (CAA) causes lobar hemorrhages and associates with Alzheimer disease. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: Parkinson pathology is alpha-synuclein in neurons (Lewy bodies), not vascular amyloid; CAA causes hemorrhage/dementia, not parkinsonism. ✅ Correct if: recurrent lobar ICH in an elderly patient.

reasoning: Bradykinesia + asymmetric rest tremor + rigidity (with REM sleep change, constipation) = Parkinson disease
Parkinson disease is loss of dopaminergic NEURONS in the substantia nigra (alpha-synuclein/Lewy bodies)
Prior significant TBI accelerates this neurodegeneration, explaining onset at age 40
mechanism: Parkinson disease is a synucleinopathy in which dopaminergic NEURONS of the substantia nigra pars compacta progressively degenerate, accumulating intracytoplasmic alpha-synuclein (Lewy bodies). Loss of nigrostriatal dopamine produces the cardinal motor signs - bradykinesia, asymmetric resting tremor, and cogwheel rigidity - often preceded by nonmotor features such as REM sleep behavior disorder and constipation, all present here. Moderate-to-severe traumatic brain injury, particularly with prolonged loss of consciousness, is an established risk factor that increases the incidence and advances the age of onset of Parkinson disease through accelerated neurodegenerative and neurotoxic cascades, accounting for parkinsonism in a 40-year-old. The other options describe glial, vascular, or amyloid processes that underlie different diseases.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Neuro > Parkinson disease: loss of dopaminergic neurons in substantia nigra; alpha-synuclein/Lewy bodies; TRAP (tremor, rigidity, akinesia/bradykinesia, postural instability); REM sleep disorder & constipation prodrome; TBI as risk factor.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: The question asks for the CELLULAR explanation. Diagnose Parkinson from the triad, then choose 'degeneration of neurons' (dopaminergic), not a glial/vascular process.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on bradykinesia, cogwheel rigidity, substantia nigra, alpha-synuclein/Lewy body, oligodendrocyte, or cerebral amyloid angiopathy? Tell me which.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Because of the TBI history, vascular/endothelial (D) or astrocyte (A) injury can tempt - but those are acute trauma effects; the chronic asymmetric triad is dopaminergic NEURON loss.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Bank: Parkinson = dopaminergic NEURON degeneration (substantia nigra, Lewy bodies); oligodendrocyte loss = MS; astrocyte swelling = edema/hepatic; vascular amyloid = CAA/Alzheimer.

keywords.front: Bradykinesia + asymmetric resting tremor + cogwheel rigidity
Cell that degenerates in Parkinson disease
keywords.back: Dopaminergic NEURONS of the substantia nigra pars compacta
REM sleep behavior disorder, constipation, hyposmia
Increased incidence and earlier onset (accelerated neurodegeneration)

firstAid.detail: Neuro > dopaminergic neuron loss in substantia nigra; Lewy bodies (alpha-synuclein); TRAP signs; asymmetric onset.
firstAid.location: Neuro > dopaminergic neuron loss in substantia nigra; Lewy bodies (alpha-synuclein); TRAP signs; asymmetric onset.

firstAid.detail: Neuro/Path > astrocyte swelling (edema/hepatic), oligodendrocyte (MS), endothelial (DAI/vascular), vascular amyloid (CAA).
firstAid.location: Neuro/Path > astrocyte swelling (edema/hepatic), oligodendrocyte (MS), endothelial (DAI/vascular), vascular amyloid (CAA).

firstAid.detail: Neuro > TBI risk factor; REM sleep behavior disorder, constipation, hyposmia precede motor signs.
firstAid.location: Neuro > TBI risk factor; REM sleep behavior disorder, constipation, hyposmia precede motor signs.

memoryHook: Parkinson kills the black-substance NEURONS (substantia nigra). A bad head injury speeds the clock - that's why this triad shows up at 40, not 70.
